grammarLessons.name: 有 & 没有 — Have / Don't Have

cards.grammarNote:
**头** (tóu) = head. Also used as a suffix to form nouns: **石头** (shítou, stone), **骨头** (gǔtou, bone).
**头疼** (tóuténg) = headache — body part + **疼** is the standard Chinese pattern for aches: **肚子疼** (stomachache), **腿疼** (leg pain).
In 我头疼, no possession marker **的** is needed — in Chinese, body parts used as topics are naturally understood as belonging to the subject.

cards.phraseRomanized: tā yǒu yī tóu cháng fà.
cards.grammarNote:
**头发** (tóufa) = head hair, where **头** (head) + **发** (grow/hair). **发** alone means 'to grow' or 'to send out' — **头发** specifies the hair that grows from the head.
**一头** is a special measure word meaning 'a head of (hair)' — used only for hair: 一头长发 (a head of long hair). For animals, **一头** means 'one head of cattle/pig.'
Note: **发** in 头发 is often pronounced with a neutral tone (tóufa), not the 4th tone (fà) it carries alone.

**脸** (liǎn) = face. Related: **脸色** (liǎnsè) = complexion/face color, **脸颊** (liǎnjiá) = cheek.
**洗脸** is a verb-object compound meaning 'wash (one's) face'. Chinese often omits the possessive pronoun for routine actions on one's own body: **洗脸** (wash face), **刷牙** (brush teeth), **梳头** (comb hair).
Note: **脸** has the **月** (flesh) radical on the left — many body-related characters share this radical.

cards.phraseRomanized: tā yǒu yī shuāng piàoliang de yǎnjing.
cards.grammarNote:
**眼睛** (yǎnjing) = eyes. **眼** (yǎn) = eye; **睛** (jīng) = pupil/eyeball. Both characters contain the **目** (eye) radical.
Alone, **眼** can refer to the eye, but **眼睛** is the full everyday word.
**一双** (yī shuāng) is the measure word for paired things: **一双眼睛** (a pair of eyes), **一双手** (a pair of hands), **一双鞋** (a pair of shoes).
**睛** is only used in eye-related words, so whenever you see **睛** you know it relates to eyes.

**耳朵** (ěrduo) = ear. **耳** is the character for ear and is itself a pictograph of an ear shape. **朵** is a suffix (originally a measure word for flowers) added to make it sound more natural.
**受伤** (shòushāng) = to get injured / be hurt — a compound verb: **受** (receive) + **伤** (wound). The **了** at the end signals a change of state (he is now injured).
Paired body parts like ears: for 'both ears' say **两只耳朵** — **只** (zhī) is the measure word for paired body parts.

**鼻子** (bízi) = nose. The character **鼻** is a pictograph — the upper part shows a self-referential 'self' radical (**自**, which also means 'self' and was originally a drawing of a nose) with the lower element **畀** indicating sound. Chinese people historically pointed to their nose to indicate 'me,' hence **自** (self) and **鼻** (nose) share history.
**子** (zi) is a diminutive/nominalizing suffix on many body part words: **鼻子** (nose), **脖子** (neck), **肚子** (belly), **耳朵** (ear uses **朵** similarly).
**堵了** = blocked/stuffed up, **了** shows it's a new state.

**嘴巴** (zuǐba) = mouth. **嘴** alone is also used (especially in compounds like **嘴唇** lips). **巴** is a suffix making it sound more colloquial — similar to **子**.
**张开** (zhāngkāi) = open (wide). **张** specifically means to spread/open outward, used mainly for mouth and similar openings. Compare **开门** (open a door, using **开** alone) vs. **张开嘴** (open your mouth).
**嘴** contains the **口** (mouth) radical, which appears in many speech/mouth-related characters.

**嘴唇** (zuǐchún) = lips. **嘴** (mouth) + **唇** (lip). **唇** has the **口** (mouth) radical at the bottom.
In Chinese, adjectives describing a state don't need a linking verb like 'is' — instead, **很** (very) serves as a slight intensifier and connector: 嘴唇很干 = 'lips are dry'. Dropping **很** would sound like a comparison ('her lips are dry, (unlike someone else's)').
Body parts in Chinese are naturally plural in meaning: **嘴唇** refers to both lips.

**牙齿** (yáchǐ) = teeth (formal/general). In everyday speech, just **牙** (yá) is common: **刷牙** (brush teeth), **牙疼** (toothache), **牙医** (dentist).
**刷牙** is a verb-object compound: **刷** (scrub/brush) + **牙** (tooth). The compound is used as a unit — to add a duration, the verb is repeated: 刷牙刷两分钟 (brush teeth for two minutes).
The **牙** character contains the tooth shape in its structure — it is a pictographic character.

cards.phraseRomanized: shíwù tàng le wǒ de shétou.
cards.grammarNote:
**舌头** (shétou) = tongue. **舌** (shé) is the character for tongue, originally a pictograph. **头** here is a nominalizing suffix (same as in **石头** stone, **骨头** bone) — it lightens the tone.
**烫** (tàng) = to scald / burn with heat. Different from **烧** (shāo, to burn with fire) — **烫** specifically means burning from hot liquids or surfaces.
**了** after **烫** indicates completion of the action: the food scalded the tongue (and it happened).

**脖子** (bózi) = neck. **脖** has the **月** (flesh) radical on the left — shared with many body-part characters: **脸** (face), **胸** (chest), **腿** (leg), **背** (back), **腰** (waist).
**子** (zi) is the diminutive/nominalizing suffix: **脖子** (neck), **鼻子** (nose), **肚子** (belly).
**僵** (jiāng) = stiff / rigid. Used for stiff muscles or joints: **脖子僵** (stiff neck), **手僵了** (hands have gone stiff, e.g., from cold).

**手臂** (shǒubì) = arm. **手** (hand) + **臂** (upper arm). **臂** contains the **月** (flesh) radical.
**摔断了** is a verb + resultative complement structure: **摔** (fall/drop) + **断** (break/snap) + **了** (completed). This pattern describes an action and its result: **摔断** (fall and break), **撞伤** (bump and injure), **切破** (cut and pierce).
For paired body parts: **一只手臂** (one arm) — **只** (zhī) is the measure word for individual items of a pair.

**手** (shǒu) = hand. A pictographic character — the original form depicted five fingers. Very productive: **手指** (finger), **手臂** (arm), **手腕** (wrist), **手套** (gloves), **手机** (mobile phone — 'hand machine').
Chinese doesn't mark singular/plural — **手** can mean 'a hand' or 'hands' depending on context. **洗手** = wash (your) hands — both understood from context.
For measure words: **一只手** (one hand, using **只** zhī for single paired body parts), **一双手** (a pair of hands).

**手指** (shǒuzhǐ) = finger. **手** (hand) + **指** (point/finger). Individual fingers: **大拇指** (thumb), **食指** (index finger), **中指** (middle finger), **无名指** (ring finger), **小指** (pinky).
**割破了** = verb + resultative complement: **割** (cut) + **破** (pierce/break through) + **了** (completed). **破** as a complement indicates something was pierced or torn: 割破 (cut through skin), 碰破 (knock and scrape), 戳破 (poke and pierce).
**脚趾** (jiǎozhǐ) = toe — same **趾** character but with the **foot** radical instead of **hand**.

cards.englishPhrase: He felt a pain in his chest.
cards.grammarNote:
**胸部** (xiōngbù) = chest (with **部** suffix meaning 'area/region'). **胸** alone is also used colloquially. **胸** contains the **月** (flesh radical) — shared with **腿** (leg), **胃** (stomach), **脑** (brain), **腰** (waist), **背** (back).
**感到** (gǎndào) = to feel / sense. **感** (feel) + **到** (arrive at/reach) — together meaning 'sensation reaches one'. Similar: **看到** (see), **听到** (hear), **闻到** (smell).
**疼痛** (téngtòng) = pain (more formal than **疼** alone). Both characters mean pain; combining them gives a stronger or more clinical tone.

cards.englishPhrase: My back hurts after sitting all day.
cards.phraseRomanized: zuò le yītiān wǒ de bèi hěn téng.
cards.grammarNote:
**背** (bèi) = back (body). Also pronounced **bēi** meaning 'to carry on one's back': **背包** (backpack). The tone changes the meaning.
**背** contains the **月** (flesh) radical at the bottom, shared with other body-part characters.
The sentence opens with a time clause: 坐了一天 (having sat for a whole day) + main clause (my back hurts). This fronted time/condition clause is very natural in Chinese.
**上背部** = upper back; **下背部** / **腰部** = lower back.

cards.englishPhrase: Exercise is good for your heart.
cards.grammarNote:
**心脏** (xīnzàng) = heart (the organ). **心** (xīn) alone = heart/mind (emotionally), used in many words: **心情** (mood), **开心** (happy), **心疼** (heartache). **脏** (zàng) = internal organ — also used in **肝脏** (liver), **肾脏** (kidney).
Note: **脏** has two pronunciations: **zàng** (organ) and **zāng** (dirty). Same character, different tones and meanings!
**对…有好处** = 'be good for…' — a fixed pattern: 运动对心脏有好处 (exercise is good for the heart).

The vocabulary word is **胃** (wèi) = stomach (the organ), but the example phrase uses **肚子** (dùzi) = belly/abdomen — the more colloquial term for stomachaches.
**胃** has the **月** (flesh) radical at the bottom. **胃口** (wèikǒu) = appetite (literally 'stomach mouth').
**肚子** = **肚** (belly, also with **月** radical) + **子** (diminutive suffix). **肚子疼** is the everyday phrase for stomachache. Note: no verb 'is' is needed — body part + **疼** directly expresses pain.

cards.englishPhrase: My muscles are sore after the workout.
cards.phraseRomanized: duànliàn hòu wǒ de jīròu hěn suān.
cards.grammarNote:
**肌肉** (jīròu) = muscle. Both **肌** and **肉** contain or represent the flesh/meat concept. **肉** alone means 'meat/flesh' and is also the radical (**月**) found in most body-part characters: **脑** (brain), **背** (back), **胸** (chest), **腿** (leg), **腰** (waist).
**酸** (suān) = sore/achy (the muscle-soreness feeling), literally 'sour'. In Chinese, sore muscles feel 'sour': **肌肉酸** or **腿酸**. This is different from **疼** (sharp pain) or **痛** (aching pain).
**锻炼后** = after exercise — **后** (hòu) as a standalone time word means 'after/afterwards'.

**腰** (yāo) = waist/lower back. Contains the **月** (flesh) radical. **腰部** (yāobù) = the waist area (more formal).
**扭腰** = to twist/strain one's waist. **扭** (niǔ) = to twist, also used in **扭伤** (sprain): **扭伤了脚踝** (sprained the ankle).
The **了** here signals completion: he twisted his waist (and that's what happened). This is the action-completion **了**, placed between verb and object when the object follows: **扭了腰** (twisted the waist).

cards.englishPhrase: Drink water to keep your skin healthy.
cards.phraseRomanized: duō hē shuǐ bǎochí pífū jiànkāng.
cards.grammarNote:
**皮肤** (pífū) = skin. **皮** (pí) = skin/leather/hide (outer layer); **肤** (fū) = skin (bodily). **皮** alone is used in: **皮鞋** (leather shoes), **皮包** (leather bag).
**多** + verb = 'do more of': **多喝水** (drink more water), **多休息** (rest more), **多运动** (exercise more). The opposite is **少** (shǎo): **少吃糖** (eat less sugar).
The sentence has no connecting word between **多喝水** and **保持皮肤健康** — in Chinese, two clauses can follow each other with an implied 'so that/to': drink more water [in order to] keep skin healthy.

**血** (xuè/xiě) = blood — the everyday word. **血液** (xuèyè) is the formal/medical term (literally 'blood fluid'), used in clinical contexts.
Note: **血** has two pronunciations — **xuè** (formal/medical, as used here) and **xiě** (colloquial). Both are correct.
**抽血** (chōu xuè) = draw blood (literally 'extract blood'). **抽** means to draw/extract: **抽烟** (smoke — draw smoke), **抽奖** (draw a prize).
**一些** = some/a few (used for uncountable or vague quantities).

**大脑** (dànǎo) = brain (formal). **脑子** (nǎozi) = brain (colloquial, with **子** suffix). **脑** contains the **月** (flesh) radical — as do most internal organ/body characters.
**动动** is a reduplicated verb — reduplicating a verb in Chinese softens the action or implies doing it 'a little bit/casually': **动动脑子** (use your brain a bit / get your brain moving), **看看** (take a look), **想想** (think about it).
**大脑** literally = 'big brain' — **大** + **脑**. Compare **小脑** (xiǎonǎo) = cerebellum (literally 'small brain').

cards.englishPhrase: The X-ray showed no skull fracture.
cards.phraseRomanized: X guāng xiǎnshì tóugǔ méiyǒu gǔzhé.
cards.grammarNote:
**头骨** (tóugǔ) = skull. **头** (head) + **骨** (bone). **骨** alone = bone, used in: **骨头** (bone, with suffix), **骨折** (fracture — 'bone break'), **骨架** (skeleton/frame).
**没有** (méiyǒu) + noun = 'do not have / there is no': 没有骨折 (no fracture). **没有** is also the negation of past actions: **他没有来** (he did not come).
**骨折** = fracture: **骨** (bone) + **折** (snap/break). Also used as a verb: **骨折了** (suffered a fracture).

**腿** (tuǐ) = leg. Contains the **月** (flesh) radical — shared by many body-part characters. **腿** refers to the whole leg; **大腿** (thigh) and **小腿** (lower leg/calf) specify parts.
Chinese doesn't distinguish singular/plural — **腿** can mean 'leg' or 'legs'. Context (or measure words) clarifies: **一条腿** (one leg) — **条** (tiáo) is the measure word for long, flexible things.
**累** (lèi) = tired (physically fatigued). Different from **困** (kùn, sleepy). After exercise: legs feel **酸** (achy/sore) or **累** (tired/fatigued).

cards.phraseRomanized: zǒulù zǒu de wǒ de jiǎo téng.
cards.grammarNote:
**脚** (jiǎo) = foot/feet. Contains the **足** (foot) radical. Related: **脚趾** (toe), **脚踝** (ankle), **脚跟** (heel).
The phrase uses a degree complement with **得** (de): **走路走得我的脚疼** = 'walked to the degree that my feet hurt'. Pattern: Verb + **得** + result/degree. When a verb has an object, repeat the verb: **走路走得…**
Measure word: **一只脚** (one foot), **一双脚** (a pair of feet).

cards.phraseRomanized: tā de jiǎozhǐ pèngdào dōngxi le.
cards.grammarNote:
**脚趾** (jiǎozhǐ) = toe. **脚** (foot) + **趾** (toe). **趾** contains the **足** (foot) radical. Compare **手指** (shǒuzhǐ, finger) — **指** has the **手** (hand) radical instead.
**碰到** (pèngdào) = to bump into / hit against. **碰** (bump) + **到** (reach/arrive at, resultative complement). The **到** signals the action reached its target.
**了** at the sentence end signals a new situation or change: 'something has happened' — she stubbed her toe (and now there's a result).

cards.phraseRomanized: tā de xī guānjié zhǒng le.
cards.grammarNote:
**关节** (guānjié) = joint. **关** = close/connect; **节** = section/segment. Together: a connecting section = joint. **节** also appears in **季节** (season) and **节日** (holiday/festival).
**膝** (xī) = knee — giving **膝关节** (knee joint) and **膝盖** (kneecap). **膝** contains the **月** (flesh) radical.
**肿了** = has become swollen. **肿** (zhǒng) = swollen; **了** signals the new state. Common with injuries: **肿了** (swollen up), **破了** (broken/cut), **瘀了** (bruised).

**呼吸** (hūxī) = to breathe / breath. **呼** = exhale (breathe out); **吸** = inhale (breathe in). Together they form the complete breathing cycle — a nice pair of opposites.
**一口气** (yī kǒu qì) = one breath (literally 'one mouthful of air'). **口** (kǒu) is the measure word for mouthfuls/breaths. **一口气** also idiomatically means 'in one go/without stopping': **一口气跑完了** (ran the whole way without stopping).
Both **呼** and **吸** contain the **口** (mouth) radical — reflecting that breathing involves the mouth/throat.

cards.englishPhrase: I can't see without my glasses.
cards.phraseRomanized: méiyǒu yǎnjìng wǒ kàn bú jiàn.
cards.grammarNote:
**看见** (kànjiàn) = to see / catch sight of. **看** (kàn) = to look/watch (the action); **见** (jiàn) = to perceive/encounter (the result). Together: the act of looking + the result of seeing.
**看不见** = cannot see — the potential negative form: Verb + **不** + complement. Compare **看得见** (can see). This pattern: V + **得/不** + complement expresses potential ability.
**眼镜** (yǎnjìng) = glasses — **眼** (eye) + **镜** (mirror/lens). Compare **眼睛** (yǎnjing, eyes) — the characters look similar but are different!

**听见** (tīngjiàn) = to hear. Parallel to **看见** (see) — **听** (listen, the action) + **见** (perceive, the result). The **见** result complement makes these sense verbs mean 'successfully perceived'.
**能** (néng) = can, expresses physical/situational capability. Compare: **会** (huì) = can, learned skill or prediction; **可以** (kěyǐ) = may, permitted or possible.
**吗** (ma) at sentence end turns a statement into a yes/no question — the simplest question formation in Chinese.

cards.phraseRomanized: wǒ néng wéndào shíwù de wèidào.
cards.grammarNote:
**闻** (wén) = to smell (sniff/detect an odor). **闻到** = to smell successfully (**到** as resultative complement indicating the sensory result was achieved). Compare **看到** (see), **听到** (hear), **感到** (feel).
**闻** contains the **耳** (ear) radical inside **门** (gate) — the original meaning was 'to hear'; in modern Chinese it shifted to mean 'smell', while **听** took over 'hear'.
**味道** (wèidào) = flavor/smell/taste. **味** = taste/flavor; **道** = way/path (extended meaning: quality/essence). **味道** covers both smell and taste in Chinese.

**尝** (cháng) = to taste (everyday, casual). The more formal term is **品尝** (pǐncháng), used in culinary or literary contexts — avoid it in everyday speech.
**我尝一下** = let me have a taste. **一下** after a verb softens the action and suggests doing it briefly: **看一下** (take a quick look), **等一下** (wait a moment), **试一下** (give it a try).
**尝一尝** is an alternative reduplication pattern with the same meaning: **尝** (taste) + **一** + **尝** → taste a little.

**触摸** (chùmō) = to touch (formal/written). In everyday speech, **碰** (pèng) or **摸** (mō) are more common: **碰** = bump/touch accidentally or lightly; **摸** = feel/stroke (more deliberate).
**不要** (bù yào) = don't (prohibition). Softer than **别** (bié) in some contexts, but both work: **别碰那个** / **不要碰那个** (don't touch that). **不要** literally = 'do not want to' but functions as an imperative negative.
**那个** = that (thing). **那** (that) + **个** (general measure word).

**肩膀** (jiānbǎng) = shoulder. **肩** (jiān) alone also means shoulder; **膀** = upper arm/shoulder area. Together they make the full word for shoulder in everyday speech.
**膀** contains the **月** (flesh) radical — the same radical in **腿** (leg), **胸** (chest), **脑** (brain), **背** (back), and dozens of other body-part characters.
Body part + **疼** (téng) = it hurts there. No copula needed: **肩膀疼** = 'the shoulder hurts'. For more intensity, add **很**: **肩膀很疼**.

**膝盖** (xīgài) = knee. **膝** (xī) = knee, contains **月** (flesh) radical. **盖** (gài) = cover/lid — the kneecap 'covers' the knee joint.
**受伤了** = got injured / was hurt. **受** (receive) + **伤** (wound/injury) + **了** (new state). This compound verb appears often in injury contexts: **受伤** (be injured), **受伤了** (has been injured now).
For knee-related words: **膝关节** (xī guānjié, knee joint), **膝盖骨** (kneecap), **护膝** (knee pad/brace).

**肘部** (zhǒubù) = elbow. **肘** (zhǒu) is the character for elbow; **部** (bù) = area/part (a suffix specifying a body region). Also said as just **肘** alone.
**撞到了** = bumped into / hit (with result). **撞** (zhuàng) = to collide/bump; **到** = resultative complement (the bump made contact); **了** = completed. This is a common injury-describing pattern: 撞到了, 碰到了, 摔到了.
**肘** appears in the idiom **肘腋之变** (a crisis from close quarters) — but in daily life just means elbow!

**眨眼** (zhǎyǎn) = to blink. **眨** (zhǎ) = to blink; contains the **目** (eye) radical.
**眨了眨** is the verb + **了** + verb reduplication pattern, conveying a brief, casual, or single occurrence of the action: 'blinked (once/a little)'. Compare **看了看** (took a look), **笑了笑** (smiled briefly).
**一眨眼** (yī zhǎ yǎn) = 'in the blink of an eye' — an idiom meaning 'in an instant': 一眨眼就不见了 (gone in the blink of an eye).

cards.englishPhrase: I sweat a lot when I exercise.
cards.phraseRomanized: wǒ yùndòng shí chū hěnduō hàn.
cards.grammarNote:
**出汗** (chūhàn) = to sweat (literally 'emit sweat'). **出** (chū) = exit/emit; **汗** (hàn) = sweat. This 'emit body substance' pattern is common: **流血** (bleed — flow blood), **流泪** (shed tears — flow tears), **流鼻涕** (have a runny nose).
**运动时** = when exercising. **时** (shí) added after a verb/noun = 'when (doing)': **吃饭时** (when eating), **睡觉时** (when sleeping).
In 出很多汗, the object **汗** is split from the compound 出汗 by the quantity **很多** — this splitting is normal when quantifying the object.

**流血** (liúxuè) = to bleed (literally 'flow blood'). **流** = flow; **血** = blood. The same pattern as **出汗** (sweat) and **流泪** (shed tears).
**在** (zài) before a verb = ongoing action (like '-ing' in English): **在流血** = is bleeding. This is one of several ways to mark ongoing action: **在**V, **正在**V, or V**着** (zhe).
**手指** = finger — **手** (hand) + **指** (point/finger). The **指** character itself can mean 'to point' as a verb.

**感觉** (gǎnjué) = to feel / feeling / sensation. Can be a verb ('I feel…') or noun ('a feeling'): **我感觉累** (I feel tired) / **这种感觉很好** (this feeling is good).
Compare: **感觉** (general feeling/sensation), **感到** (feel/sense something specific, often emotional), **觉得** (juéde, think/feel — often expressing opinion or mild sensation).
**累** (lèi) = tired/fatigued. Different from **困** (kùn) = sleepy. After physical exertion, use **累**: 我很累 (I'm tired).

**思考** (sīkǎo) = to think/reflect (more deliberate than casual thinking). Compare: **想** (xiǎng) = think/want (casual, everyday); **思考** = think deeply/consider carefully.
**说话前** = before speaking. Time expressions with **前** (before) and **后** (after) come before the main clause: **说话前先思考** (before speaking, first think).
**先** (xiān) = first/before anything else. Often paired with **再** (zài, then): **先想，再说** (first think, then speak).

cards.phraseRomanized: màn màn yídòng nǐ de shǒubì.
cards.grammarNote:
**移动** (yídòng) = to move (an object or body part). **移** = shift/transfer; **动** = move/motion. For moving one's own body parts in everyday speech, **动** alone is also common: **动一动手臂** (move your arm around).
**慢慢** (mànmàn) = slowly. Reduplicated adjective used as an adverb: **慢慢走** (walk slowly), **慢慢来** (take your time — a common reassurance). Reduplication of monosyllabic adjectives typically adds a gentler, more gradual quality.
**手臂** = arm: **手** (hand) + **臂** (upper arm). The **臂** character contains the **月** flesh radical.

**骨感** (gǔgǎn) = bony/angular (literally 'bone-feeling'). **骨** (bone) + **感** (sensation/feeling). In modern Chinese, **骨感** can be a compliment (slender, model-like figure) or a description of being thin/bony.
**看起来** (kàn qǐlái) = to look / appear. A very useful fixed phrase: **看起来很累** (looks tired), **看起来很年轻** (looks young). The **起来** directional complement adds the meaning of 'upon looking/in appearance'.
**骨** (gǔ) appears in: **骨头** (bone), **头骨** (skull), **骨折** (fracture), **骨架** (skeleton).

cards.phraseRomanized: tā de shǒubì hěn duō máo.
cards.grammarNote:
**多毛** (duō máo) = hairy (literally 'lots of hair/fur'). **毛** (máo) = body hair, fur, feathers (shorter/finer hair, unlike **发** which is head hair). **毛** also means 'cent' (money) in colloquial speech.
Distinguish: **头发** (tóufa) = head hair; **毛发** (máofà) = hair/fur (general); **眉毛** (éyebrow hair); **睫毛** (eyelashes).
**很多** typically means 'a lot of' — here **很多毛** = a lot of body hair, describing the state as having much fur/hair.

cards.englishPhrase: A baby's skin is very soft.
cards.grammarNote:
**软** (ruǎn) = soft. Opposite: **硬** (yìng) = hard. Both describe textures and also figurative qualities: **软件** (software — 'soft piece'), **硬件** (hardware — 'hard piece').
**婴儿** (yīng'ér) = infant/baby. The apostrophe in the romanization marks a syllable boundary (ying + er), preventing **yinger** from being read as **yin + ger**.
**皮肤** = skin. Note that **很** before an adjective in a descriptive sentence is not truly 'very' — it's a weak intensifier that sounds more natural than a bare adjective. **皮肤软** (without 很) would imply comparison.

cards.englishPhrase: My muscles are sore after exercising.
cards.grammarNote:
**酸痛** (suāntòng) = sore/achy (the dull post-exercise feeling). **酸** = sour/achy; **痛** = pain. Together: the 'sour-aching' sensation typical of muscle soreness.
Three key pain words: **疼** (téng, general hurt/ache), **痛** (tòng, sharper or more intense pain), **酸痛** (suāntòng, the achy-sour soreness after exercise).
**运动完** = after finishing exercise. **完** as a resultative complement: **运动完** = finished exercising. This time expression fronts the sentence naturally in Chinese.

**左** (zuǒ) = left; **右** (yòu) = right. A mnemonic: **左** has **工** (work) on the right side of the character — 'most people work with their right hand, so the work-side is the opposite of left.' Made-up but memorable!
**举起** (jǔqǐ) = raise/lift up. **举** = raise; **起** = directional complement (upward motion).
**左手** (left hand), **右手** (right hand), **左脚** (left foot), **右脚** (right foot) — direction + body part is the standard pattern.

cards.englishPhrase: Turn your head to the right.
cards.grammarNote:
**把** (bǎ) is the disposal/manipulation marker — it moves the object before the verb to emphasize how it is handled/affected: **把头转向右边** (turn the head toward the right). **把** sentences require a specific, actionable result.
**转向** (zhuǎn xiàng) = turn toward. **转** = turn/rotate; **向** = toward/facing. **右边** = right side: **右** (right) + **边** (side). Similarly: **左边** (left side), **上边** (top), **下边** (bottom).
**把** + object + verb pattern is essential in Chinese for expressing deliberate manipulation of an object.

cards.englishPhrase: She has pain in her upper back.
cards.grammarNote:
**上** (shàng) = upper/above; **下** (xià) = lower/below. As prefixes for body areas: **上背部** (upper back), **下背部** (lower back), **上肢** (upper limbs), **下肢** (lower limbs).
**背部** = the back region. **部** (bù) = area/part/section — added to body parts to specify the region: **胸部** (chest area), **腰部** (waist area), **腿部** (leg area).
**疼痛** (téngtòng) = pain. More formal than **疼** alone, often used in medical contexts. Both **疼** and **痛** mean pain — combining them intensifies or formalizes the meaning.

**下** (xià) = lower/below. Opposite of **上** (shàng, upper). **下部** = lower part/section.
Interestingly, the example phrase uses **腰部** (waist area) rather than **下背部** (lower back) — in Chinese, lower back pain is typically described as **腰疼** or **腰痛** rather than specifying 'lower'. **腰** naturally refers to the lumbar region.
**腰部** = waist/lower back area. **部** adds the sense of 'the region of'. Pattern: body part + **部** = the [body part] area: **腰部** (waist region), **肩部** (shoulder region), **颈部** (neck region).

**身体** (shēntǐ) = body (physical health and body). **身** = torso/self; **体** = body/form. Used in greetings: **身体好吗？** (How is your health?). Compare **身材** (shēncái) = figure/physique (shape of body).
**爱护** (àihù) = take care of / cherish and protect. **爱** (love) + **护** (protect). Stronger than just **照顾** (zhàogù, look after) — implies both caring and guarding.
**身** alone appears in: **身高** (height), **身份** (identity), **身边** (by one's side).

**人类** (rénlèi) = humankind/humanity. **人** (person) + **类** (type/category). **人类的** = 'of humanity' / 'human (adj.)'. Compare: **人** alone (a person), **人们** (rénmen, people in general), **人类** (humankind as a species).
**大脑** = brain: **大** (big) + **脑** (brain). **脑** contains the **月** (flesh) radical. The character **脑** can be broken down as flesh (月) + the brain structure on top.
**神奇** (shénqí) = amazing/miraculous. **神** = spirit/divine; **奇** = strange/wondrous. Together: wonderfully astonishing.
